claim: Lost dogs with xenophobic (fearful) temperaments travel farther, avoid all humans including owners, and are at higher risk of vehicle strikes.
stance_target: temperament as primary predictor of search radius
scope_conditions: Lost dogs across varied terrain types, Kat Albrecht MAR Network empirical observation since 1997
quoted_support: Dogs with xenophobic temperaments (due to genetics and/or puppyhood experiences) are more inclined to travel farther and are at a higher risk of being hit by cars.

claim: Blind panic from loud noises (fireworks, gunfire, thunder) causes dogs to run for several miles at maximum speed before slowing.
stance_target: noise-triggered panic as longest-distance dispersal mechanism
scope_conditions: Dogs of any temperament exposed to sudden loud stimuli
quoted_support: A dog that bolts in panic due to fireworks or thunder will take off at a blind run and can run for several miles.

claim: Terrain acts as a movement barrier: fences, cactus, heavy brush, and steep cliffs redirect a dog's path toward the line of least resistance.
stance_target: terrain corridor modeling — dogs follow least-resistance paths
quoted_support: Fences that create barriers will influence a dog's travel since a dog will tend to take the 'path of least resistance' when traveling. Cactus, heavy brush, and steep cliffs can be barriers that influence whether a dog continues on a path or changes directions.

claim: Population density is inversely correlated with travel distance: dogs escaping into high-density urban areas are typically recovered close to the escape point, while rural/mountainous escapes produce far greater travel distances.
stance_target: population density as search radius modulator
scope_conditions: All dog temperaments, escape in varied population densities
quoted_support: A dog that escapes in Manhattan will travel a shorter distance than will a dog that escapes in the Rockies or in rural farmland. When dogs escape into areas with a high number of people, their chances of being found close to the escape point are increased.

claim: Extreme weather conditions (snow, hail, rain, heat) decrease the distance a lost dog travels.
stance_target: heat and temperature effects on dog movement range
quoted_support: Extreme weather conditions (snow, hail, rain, sweltering heat) will decrease the distances that lost dogs travel.

claim: People who find stray dogs frequently transport them out of the immediate search area, making it essential to search rescue/foster networks in addition to the local area.
stance_target: vehicle transport as the primary displacement mechanism for found dogs
scope_conditions: Urban and suburban lost dog scenarios
quoted_support: One complicating factor with lost dogs is that people who pick up stray dogs often transport them out of the immediate search area.

claim: Calling a stray or panicked dog can trigger flight response, especially if multiple prior capture attempts have conditioned the dog to associate voices with threat.
scope_conditions: Panicked or previously approached lost dogs
quoted_support: If too many people have already tried to capture the dog, calling him becomes a 'trigger' that can cause him to automatically take off in fear when anyone, including his owner, calls him.

claim: Small, elderly, and disabled dogs are typically recovered within 1-2 miles of their escape point, while panicked/skittish dogs may require a 5-10 mile search radius.
scope_conditions: Lost dogs of varying size, age, and temperament
quoted_support: Aggressive dogs, panicked dogs, and dogs with skittish, shy temperaments will be more difficult to capture and are at risk of traveling further... The radius for your posters should encompass a large area – a 5 to 10-mile radius in some cases.
